reports.title: Grandparent Emergency Scam
reports.summary: Scammers call pretending to be your grandchild in trouble — arrested, in an accident, or at the hospital — and urgently need bail money or medical fees sent via gift cards or wire transfer.
safetyTips: Never send money without calling the person directly on a number you already know.
Create a secret family code word that only your real family members would know.
Real emergencies don't require gift cards — that is always a scam.
Hang up and call your grandchild's known phone number to verify.
reports.howItWorks: A scammer calls or texts pretending to be a grandchild in a sudden crisis — arrested, in a car accident, or hospitalized in another state or country. They beg the victim not to tell anyone else in the family "to avoid embarrassment," then hand the phone to a fake lawyer, bail bondsman, or doctor who pressures for immediate payment, almost always via gift cards, wire transfer, or a cash pickup courier — all of which are nearly impossible to trace or reverse.
redFlags: Caller insists on secrecy and tells you not to call other family members to verify.
Payment is demanded via gift cards, wire transfer, or a courier pickup — never a normal payment method.
The situation is manufactured to feel extremely urgent, leaving no time to think.
The voice sounds "a little different" but is explained away as a bad connection or a swollen jaw from the accident.
realExamples: A grandmother in Ohio sent $9,000 in Target gift cards after a caller claiming to be her grandson said he needed bail money after a DUI arrest.
A retired couple wired $12,000 to a "lawyer" after their granddaughter's voice appeared to describe a car accident abroad — the granddaughter was safe at home the entire time.
reports.source: FTC Consumer Alerts + VerifyGuard user-submitted reports

reports.title: AI Voice Clone Scam
reports.summary: Using just a few seconds of audio from social media, scammers clone a loved one's voice with AI and call you claiming to be in an emergency. The voice sounds completely real.
safetyTips: Establish a family safe word that AI cannot know — use it to verify identity.
Hang up and call back on a number stored in your own contacts.
Be suspicious of any caller who immediately asks for money.
Make your social media voice content private to limit what AI can clone.

reports.title: Fake IRS Tax Refund Scam
reports.summary: Calls, texts, or emails claiming you owe back taxes and will be arrested, or that you are owed a refund that requires you to pay a processing fee first — often via gift cards.
safetyTips: The IRS never calls demanding immediate payment or gift cards.
The IRS never threatens arrest over the phone.
Verify any IRS notice by calling 1-800-829-1040 directly.
The IRS contacts taxpayers by mail first, never phone or email.

reports.title: Tech Support Pop-Up Scam
reports.summary: A scary browser pop-up claims your computer is infected with viruses and displays a 'Microsoft' or 'Apple' phone number. Calling it connects you to scammers who request remote access to your computer.
safetyTips: Never call a phone number shown in a browser pop-up.
Legitimate companies like Microsoft and Apple do not make unsolicited support calls.
Close the browser tab and restart your browser to clear the pop-up.
Never allow remote access to your computer from an unsolicited caller.

reports.title: Romance / Military Scam
reports.summary: Fake online dating profiles — often claiming to be deployed military, offshore oil workers, or traveling doctors — build a relationship over weeks before creating a financial crisis requiring money.
safetyTips: Never send money to someone you have not met in person.
Do a reverse image search on their profile photos — they are often stolen.
Be suspicious of anyone who refuses to video chat or always cancels.
Deployed US military personnel have access to military banking and don't need money wired.

reports.title: Crypto Investment Scam
reports.summary: Promises of guaranteed high returns on cryptocurrency or forex investments, often introduced through social media, fake apps, or romance relationships. Victims lose everything when they try to withdraw.
safetyTips: No legitimate investment guarantees returns — that claim is always a lie.
Verify any investment firm with the SEC at investor.gov or FINRA at BrokerCheck.
Never invest money you cannot afford to lose completely.
If someone you met online is suggesting investments, that is a major red flag.

reports.title: Medicare / Health Insurance Scam
reports.summary: Callers pretend to be from Medicare or a health insurance company offering free equipment or coverage updates, then steal your Medicare number to commit fraud and bill for services you never received.
safetyTips: Medicare will never call you asking for your Medicare number.
Guard your Medicare number like a credit card number.
Review your Medicare Summary Notice for charges you don't recognize.
Report suspected fraud to 1-800-MEDICARE.

reports.title: Lottery / Prize Notification Scam
reports.summary: You receive a letter, call, or email claiming you've won a lottery or sweepstakes you never entered. To claim the prize you must first pay taxes, fees, or customs charges upfront.
safetyTips: You cannot win a contest you never entered.
Legitimate prizes never require upfront fees or taxes to claim.
Winning notifications are never sent by email, phone, or letter.
The only real lottery is one you purchased a ticket for yourself.

reports.title: Wangiri / One-Ring Callback Scam
reports.summary: A call rings once from an unfamiliar international number and hangs up before you can answer, banking on curiosity making you call back. The returned call connects to a premium-rate line that racks up enormous per-minute charges, a cut of which goes to the scammer.
safetyTips: Don't call back unfamiliar international numbers, especially after just one ring.
Check the country code before dialing back — unrecognized codes are a red flag.
Block and report the number instead of returning the call.
Contact your carrier if you're billed for a call you never intentionally placed.

reports.title: "Can You Hear Me?" Robocall Scam
reports.summary: A robocall or live scammer opens with a simple yes-or-no question like "can you hear me?" hoping to record you saying "yes," which they claim can later be used to authorize charges you never agreed to.
safetyTips: Simply hang up on robocalls instead of engaging, even to say "no."
Answer with a neutral greeting rather than "yes" or "no" if you must respond.
Never confirm personal details to an unsolicited caller, recorded or not.
Register your number on the National Do Not Call Registry to cut down on robocalls.

reports.title: Fake Bank Fraud Alert Scam
reports.summary: A text or call appears to come from your bank warning of suspicious activity and asks you to "verify" your account by providing your card number, PIN, or a one-time passcode — information a real bank never requests this way.
safetyTips: Hang up and call the number on the back of your card, never a number from the text or call itself.
Never share a one-time passcode with anyone, even someone claiming to be your bank.
Banks never ask for your full PIN or password over phone or text.
Log in directly through your bank's official app or website to check for real alerts.

reports.title: Jury Duty Scam
reports.summary: A caller claims to be a sheriff or court officer saying you missed jury duty and now face arrest unless you immediately pay a fine — usually via gift cards or a prepaid card — to avoid being taken into custody.
safetyTips: Courts never demand payment over the phone to avoid arrest.
Gift cards are never a legitimate form of payment for any government fee or fine.
Hang up and call your local courthouse directly using a number you look up yourself.
Law enforcement does not call ahead to warn of an imminent arrest.

reports.title: Social Security Number Suspended Scam
reports.summary: An automated call or voicemail claims your Social Security number has been "suspended" due to suspicious activity and instructs you to press a number or call back, aiming to extract your SSN and other personal details.
safetyTips: The Social Security Administration cannot suspend your number and will never call to tell you so.
Never give your Social Security number to an unsolicited caller.
Hang up and call the SSA directly using their official number to check your account status.
Report suspicious calls to the SSA Office of the Inspector General.

reports.title: Unemployment Benefits Identity Theft Scam
reports.summary: Criminals use stolen personal information to file fraudulent unemployment claims in a victim's name, often surfacing only when the real person receives a tax form for benefits they never applied for or received.
safetyTips: Check your credit report regularly for unfamiliar accounts or inquiries.
If you receive an unexpected tax form for benefits you never claimed, report it to your state unemployment office immediately.
Freeze your credit with the three major bureaus if you suspect identity theft.
File a report with the FTC at IdentityTheft.gov if you're a victim.

reports.title: Student Loan Forgiveness Scam
reports.summary: Companies advertise guaranteed student loan forgiveness or dramatically reduced payments for an upfront fee, then either do nothing, enroll borrowers in a plan they could have gotten for free, or steal the login credentials used to "help."
safetyTips: Legitimate loan forgiveness and repayment programs are always free to apply for directly through your loan servicer.
Never pay an upfront fee for loan forgiveness or consolidation help.
Never share your Federal Student Aid (FSA) ID with a third party.
Verify any offer directly at StudentAid.gov.

reports.title: USCIS / Immigration Scam
reports.summary: Scammers posing as immigration officials contact applicants claiming a fee must be paid immediately by phone to avoid deportation or a denied application, exploiting fear around immigration status.
safetyTips: USCIS does not accept payment over the phone or via gift cards.
Government agencies communicate official actions by mail, not urgent phone calls.
Verify your case status directly on the official USCIS website.
Consult a licensed immigration attorney if you're unsure about a communication.

reports.title: Phishing Email Scam
reports.summary: A fraudulent email impersonates a trusted company or contact, urging the recipient to click a link and log in on a fake page that steals their username and password, or to open an attachment that installs malware.
safetyTips: Hover over links to check the real destination before clicking.
Look closely at the sender's actual email address, not just the display name.
Never enter your password after clicking a link in an unsolicited email.
Enable two-factor authentication so a stolen password alone isn't enough to break in.

reports.title: Smishing Text Message Scam
reports.summary: Phishing conducted over SMS text message, often impersonating a delivery service, bank, or government agency with a link to a fake site designed to steal login credentials or payment information.
safetyTips: Don't tap links in unexpected text messages, even if they look official.
Go directly to the official app or website instead of using a link from a text.
Forward suspicious texts to 7726 (SPAM) to report them, then delete.
Never enter a one-time passcode on a page you reached through a text link.

reports.title: QR Code Scam
reports.summary: A malicious QR code — placed over a legitimate one on a parking meter, menu, or poster, or sent digitally — redirects victims to a fake payment or login page designed to steal money or credentials.
safetyTips: Check for a sticker placed over another QR code before scanning in public.
Preview the destination URL before opening it, if your camera app supports it.
Avoid entering payment or login details on a page reached by scanning an untrusted QR code.
Use the official app or website directly instead of scanning when possible.

reports.title: Fake Antivirus / Scareware Scam
reports.summary: A pop-up or fake scan result falsely claims your device is riddled with viruses and pressures you to buy bogus security software or call a "support" number, which leads straight into a tech support scam.
safetyTips: Real antivirus software doesn't sell itself through browser pop-ups.
Close the browser tab or restart your device rather than clicking anything in the pop-up.
Only install security software directly from a vendor's official website.
Run a scan with security software you installed yourself if you're worried about infection.

reports.title: Social Media Account Takeover Scam
reports.summary: A hacked or cloned social media account messages the victim's friends and family asking for money, gift cards, or a "favor" verification code, exploiting the trust of an existing relationship.
safetyTips: Call or video chat to confirm identity before sending money to anyone contacting you on social media, even a close friend.
Never share a login verification code you receive by text, even if a friend asks for it.
Enable two-factor authentication on all of your social accounts.
Report and alert your contacts immediately if your account is compromised.

reports.title: SIM Swap Scam
reports.summary: A scammer convinces your mobile carrier to transfer your phone number to a SIM card they control, letting them intercept the text-message codes used to reset passwords and drain bank or crypto accounts.
safetyTips: Add a PIN or passcode to your mobile account that's required for any SIM changes.
Use an authenticator app instead of SMS for two-factor authentication where possible.
Watch for an unexpected loss of cell signal, which can indicate your number was just transferred.
Contact your carrier immediately if your phone suddenly loses service without explanation.

reports.title: Virtual Kidnapping Scam
reports.summary: A caller claims to be holding a family member hostage and demands an immediate ransom payment, sometimes using an AI-cloned voice of the "victim" crying for help, even though the person is actually safe.
safetyTips: Try to contact the supposedly kidnapped person directly on their own phone before doing anything else.
Stay calm and ask questions only the real person would know the answer to.
Never wire money or buy gift cards based on a phone threat alone.
Contact local police immediately — they have protocols for these calls.

reports.title: Deepfake Celebrity Endorsement Scam
reports.summary: AI-generated video or audio shows a celebrity or public figure appearing to endorse an investment platform, giveaway, or product they have never actually endorsed, used to lend false credibility to a scam.
safetyTips: Be skeptical of any celebrity "endorsement" for a financial product seen only in an ad or video.
Check the celebrity's own verified social accounts or official site for confirmation.
Look for subtle glitches in lip-sync, lighting, or audio that can reveal an AI fake.
Remember that real investment opportunities are never promoted through unsolicited video ads.

reports.title: AI Chatbot Romance Scam
reports.summary: An AI-powered chatbot, sometimes on a dating app, carries on a convincingly personal conversation for weeks or months before steering the victim toward sending money, gifts, or investing in a fraudulent scheme.
safetyTips: Be cautious of a match who is available to chat constantly at all hours but avoids calls or video.
Watch for oddly generic or repetitive phrasing that can indicate an automated response.
Never send money or financial details to someone you've only spoken to online.
Ask specific, personal follow-up questions that a scripted bot is likely to answer inconsistently.

reports.title: Catfishing Scam
reports.summary: Someone creates a fake online identity, often using stolen photos of an attractive stranger, to build a relationship for emotional manipulation, financial gain, or simply to deceive without necessarily asking for money.
safetyTips: Reverse image search profile photos to check if they've been stolen from someone else.
Insist on a live video call early in any online relationship.
Be wary of inconsistent details about their job, location, or life story.
Trust your instincts if something about their story doesn't add up.

reports.title: Sextortion Scam
reports.summary: A scammer coerces or tricks a victim into sharing intimate photos or video, then threatens to release them to friends, family, or publicly unless paid, often escalating demands after the first payment.
safetyTips: Stop all contact and payment — paying rarely stops the threats and often increases them.
Report the account to the platform and preserve evidence with screenshots.
Contact the National Center for Missing & Exploited Children or local law enforcement, especially if a minor is involved.
Remember you are the victim of a crime, not someone at fault for being deceived.

reports.title: Ponzi Scheme
reports.summary: An investment scheme pays early investors with money from new investors rather than actual profit, creating the illusion of a successful business until new money stops flowing in and the scheme collapses.
safetyTips: Be suspicious of consistently high returns regardless of market conditions.
Verify any investment manager is registered with the SEC or your state securities regulator.
Ask how the underlying business actually generates the returns being promised.
Be cautious of pressure to recruit new investors as part of the "opportunity."

reports.title: Pump and Dump Scam
reports.summary: Promoters hype a low-value stock or cryptocurrency through social media and unsolicited messages to inflate its price, then sell their own holdings at the peak, leaving new buyers with a worthless investment.
safetyTips: Be skeptical of unsolicited stock or crypto tips promising huge, fast gains.
Research a company's fundamentals independently before buying based on social media hype.
Watch for coordinated promotion of the same ticker across many accounts at once.
Never invest based on pressure to "buy now before it's too late."

reports.title: Fake Trading App Scam
reports.summary: Victims are convinced to download a fraudulent investment or trading app that shows fabricated, ever-increasing account balances, but withdrawals are blocked or additional "fees" are demanded before any money can be returned.
safetyTips: Only download trading apps from official app stores, and verify the developer.
Try withdrawing a small amount early to test whether the platform is legitimate.
Be suspicious of any app that requires an additional fee before allowing a withdrawal.
Verify the platform is registered with financial regulators like the SEC or CFTC.

reports.title: Pig Butchering Scam
reports.summary: Named for the practice of fattening livestock before slaughter, scammers build a long-term relationship — often starting as a romance or friendship — before gradually persuading the victim to invest increasing amounts into a fake trading platform, ultimately taking everything.
safetyTips: Be wary of a new online contact who eventually steers conversation toward an investment opportunity.
Never move funds to a trading platform recommended by someone you've only met online.
Independently verify any platform's registration before depositing money.
Cut off contact and report to authorities as soon as investment pressure begins.

reports.title: Advance Fee Loan Scam
reports.summary: A "guaranteed approval" loan offer, often targeting people with poor credit, requires an upfront fee for insurance, processing, or collateral before the loan is disbursed — but the loan never actually arrives.
safetyTips: Legitimate lenders deduct fees from the loan amount — they don't require payment upfront.
Be suspicious of any lender who guarantees approval without checking your credit.
Verify a lender is licensed to operate in your state.
Never wire money or use gift cards to pay a loan "fee."

reports.title: Fake Online Store Scam
reports.summary: A convincing but fraudulent online store, often advertised through social media, offers heavily discounted name-brand products, takes payment, and either ships nothing, a counterfeit, or a completely different item.
safetyTips: Search the store name plus "scam" or "reviews" before ordering.
Be cautious of prices that are dramatically lower than anywhere else.
Pay with a credit card rather than a debit card or wire transfer for purchase protection.
Check for a real physical address and working customer service contact.

reports.title: Online Marketplace Buyer Scam
reports.summary: A "buyer" on a marketplace app sends a fake payment confirmation screenshot or overpays with a bad check, then asks the seller to ship the item or refund the difference before the payment actually clears.
safetyTips: Confirm payment has actually landed in your account, not just a screenshot, before shipping anything.
Be suspicious of any buyer who overpays and asks for a refund of the difference.
Meet locally for cash when possible instead of shipping to unverified buyers.
Use the marketplace's built-in, verified payment system rather than outside apps.

reports.title: Fake Job Offer Scam
reports.summary: A recruiter offers a remote job with little to no interview, then asks the "new hire" to pay for training materials, equipment, or a background check upfront, or to deposit a check and wire back the difference.
safetyTips: Be suspicious of any job offer that skips a real interview process.
Never pay upfront for equipment, training, or a background check.
Research the company independently, including looking for a real office and other employees.
Never deposit a check and wire money back to an "employer."

reports.title: Mystery Shopper Check Scam
reports.summary: A fake mystery shopping job sends the victim a check to deposit, instructing them to keep a "payment" and wire the rest to evaluate a money transfer service — but the original check later bounces, leaving the victim owing the full amount.
safetyTips: Be suspicious of any job that pays you by having you deposit and forward a check.
Remember that a bank clearing a check temporarily doesn't mean it won't later be discovered as fraudulent.
Never wire money based on funds from a check you just deposited.
Verify legitimate mystery shopping companies through the Mystery Shopping Professionals Association.

reports.title: Package Delivery Text Scam
reports.summary: A text message claims a package couldn't be delivered due to an unpaid fee or incomplete address, with a link to a fake carrier site designed to steal payment card details.
safetyTips: Track packages directly through the retailer or carrier's official app or website.
Delivery carriers don't typically require a small fee by text message to redeliver a package.
Don't click links in unexpected delivery texts — go to the official site instead.
Forward suspicious delivery texts to 7726 (SPAM) to report them.

reports.title: Fake Charity / Disaster Relief Scam
reports.summary: In the wake of a natural disaster or tragedy, fraudulent charities solicit donations through calls, texts, or social media using names deceptively similar to real, established relief organizations.
safetyTips: Give directly through a charity's official website rather than through a link in a text or email.
Check a charity's legitimacy on Charity Navigator or the BBB Wise Giving Alliance.
Be wary of high-pressure requests for immediate donations right after a disaster.
Never donate using gift cards or wire transfers.

reports.title: Fake Rental Listing Scam
reports.summary: A rental listing — sometimes copied from a real property that isn't actually for rent — advertises an attractive price and asks for a deposit or first month's rent before the "landlord," who is never available to meet in person, disappears.
safetyTips: Never pay a deposit before viewing a property in person or via a verified live video call.
Search the listing photos online to check if they've been copied from another listing.
Verify property ownership through public county property records.
Be suspicious of a landlord who is always "out of the country" or unavailable to meet.
